title: Reading Files 📖
description: Connect your code to the hard drive.

slides.title: The Next Frontier 🌌
slides.content:
Variables and arrays live in RAM, meaning they are completely wiped out the second the program stops. If you want a High Score to survive after the game closes, you have to write it to the physical Hard Drive.

In C, we use `FILE` pointers to interact with the hard drive.

slides.title: Opening a File
slides.content: To read a file, we use `fopen()` with the `"r"` (Read) mode. It returns a `FILE` pointer. If the file doesn't exist, it returns `NULL`.

quizData.question: What is the very first thing you should do after calling `fopen("file.txt", "r")`?

options.text: Check if the pointer it returned is strictly equal to `NULL` to ensure the file actually existed.

quizData.explanation: If a user deletes the file and you blindly try to read from the NULL pointer, your app will instantly crash.

slides.title: Reading the Data
slides.content:
Once the file is open, you can read text from it using `fgets()` (File Get String).

`fgets` needs 3 things: The buffer to store the text in, the maximum number of characters to read safely, and the file pointer.

quizData.question: Just like `malloc` requires `free`, opening a file with `fopen` requires you to eventually use `fclose` to release the file back to the OS.
quizData.explanation: If you don't close the file, the Operating System will keep a 'Lock' on it, preventing the user or other programs from moving or deleting the file.
